---
title: "The Machinery and the Data"
date: "2026-07-20"
---

# The Machinery and the Data {.title}

::: notes
Welcome back to Chapter five-point-one, part three of seven. In part two we settled the genre — descriptive, not causal — and previewed the famous result that beta goes flat. Now we open the engine room. Two pieces of machinery drive the whole paper, and you have already built both. Then we look at the data they run on, and at one timing trick that protects the paper from a classic mistake.
:::

## The Machinery: Two Pieces You Already Built

- **Piece one — portfolio sorts.** Rank, bin into deciles, average per bucket
- Nonparametric: no linear form imposed — let the data show its shape
- The power move: the **double sort** (size *and* beta at once)
- **Piece two — Fama–MacBeth.** This is Lab 2, verbatim
- Pass 1: time-series betas. Pass 2: monthly cross-sectional regressions

::: notes
Piece one: portfolio sorts. Rather than trust a single regression line, Fama and French sort stocks into buckets and look at average returns bucket by bucket. Rank every stock by size, cut into deciles, average each decile's return — that is a univariate sort. The power move is the double sort: form a grid by sorting on size and on beta at the same time, so each cell holds firms similar on one dimension and spread on the other. Sorts are nonparametric — they impose no linear form — so the data shows its shape before any model is forced on it. Piece two: the Fama–MacBeth cross-sectional regression — Lab Two, verbatim. Pass one estimates each stock's beta from a time-series regression on the market. Pass two runs, separately in each month, a cross-sectional regression of that month's returns on the characteristics, producing a time series of monthly slope estimates.
:::

## Why the Fama–MacBeth Standard Error Is Honest

- Average the monthly slopes; get the SE *free* from their scatter
- $$\hat\gamma_j = \tfrac{1}{T}\sum_{t}\hat\gamma_{j,t},\quad \widehat{\operatorname{se}}(\hat\gamma_j)=\tfrac{\operatorname{sd}_t(\hat\gamma_{j,t})}{\sqrt{T}}$$
- Within a month, every stock feels the same shock (Ch 2.4 time effect)
- Pooling would treat correlated errors as independent — SE too small
- **Beta is an estimate** — so use *portfolio* betas, lagged

::: notes
You average each monthly slope to get the point estimate: gamma-hat-sub-j equals one over T times the sum of the monthly slopes. And you get its standard error free from the scatter — the standard deviation of the slopes divided by the square root of T. Why is this honest? In any single month, every stock is shoved by the same market-wide shock, so the within-month errors are violently correlated — the time effect of Chapter two-point-four. Pooling all stock-months into one giant regression would treat correlated observations as independent and report a standard error far too small. Fama–MacBeth dissolves that: it collapses each month to one slope, then treats the slopes as a little time series. One subtlety: a stock's beta is itself an estimate from pass one, and individual betas are noisy. So Fama and French assign betas using portfolios, estimated on data from before the return month. Hold onto "beta is an estimate" — it returns with teeth later.
:::

## The Data: CRSP + Compustat, with a Timing Gap

- **Returns from CRSP**; **book equity from Compustat** — the workhorse pair
- Sample: **nonfinancial** NYSE, AMEX, NASDAQ firms, roughly **1963–1990**
- Financials dropped — high leverage makes BE/ME non-comparable
- Returns July of $t$ → June of $t{+}1$ matched to accounting from $t{-}1$
- The gap guards against **look-ahead bias**

::: notes
Now the data — two databases stitched together. Returns come from CRSP, the Center for Research in Security Prices, with monthly returns for essentially every stock on the major U.S. exchanges. Book equity comes from Compustat, the accounting database. Size needs only price and shares, so it comes from CRSP; book-to-market needs both. The sample is nonfinancial firms on the New York, American, and NASDAQ exchanges, over roughly nineteen sixty-three to nineteen ninety. Financials are dropped because their high leverage makes book-to-market non-comparable. And here is the heart of it: returns from July of year t through June of year t-plus-one are matched to accounting data from the fiscal year ending in year t-minus-one. That deliberate gap protects against look-ahead bias. A December annual report is not public until months later, so matching it to that same December's return would use information no investor could have had, manufacturing predictability that does not exist in real time. The lag forces every explanatory variable to be knowable before the returns it explains.
:::

## Key Takeaway {.title}

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The engine is **portfolio sorts** plus the **Fama–MacBeth** regression from Lab 2, whose standard errors are honest because they collapse each month to one slope. The data is **CRSP + Compustat** — nonfinancial firms, ~1963–1990 — with a July-to-June **timing lag** that blocks look-ahead bias. Next: reading the paper's tables in the right order, starting with the sorts.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part three: the engine is two things you already own — portfolio sorts and the Fama–MacBeth regression, whose standard errors are honest because they never compare across months. The data is CRSP and Compustat, nonfinancial firms from sixty-three to ninety, with a timing lag that blocks look-ahead bias. In part four we read like a referee: tables first, watching beta die before any regression coefficient.
:::
